content:
Consider a counter-flow heat exchanger with the inlet temperatures of two fluids (1 and 2) being T1, in = 300 K and T2, in = 350 K. The heat capacity rates of the two fluids are C1 = 1000 W/K and C2 = 400 W/K, and the effectiveness of the heat exchanger is 0.5. The actual heat transfer rate is _____ kW.

(Answer in integer)
explanation:
Inlet temperature of first fluid $T_{1_{(n)}}=300 \mathrm{~K}$

Inlet temperature of second fluid $T_{2_{(i n)}}=350 \mathrm{~K}$

Heat capacity of first fluid $C_1=1000 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{k}$

Heat capacity of second fluid $C_2=400 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{k}$

Effectiveness of heat exchanger (counter flow) $\varepsilon=0.5$

As we know,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Effectiveness } \varepsilon=\frac{\text { Actual heat transfer }}{\text { maximum possible heat transfer }} \\ & 0.5=\frac{\text { Actual heat transfer }}{C_{\text {min }}\left(T_{2_{(\mathrm{min}}}-T_{1_{\text {(in) }}}\right)} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Actual heat transfer }=0.5 \times 400 \times(350-300)=10 \mathrm{~kW} $$

Hence, the correct answer is 10 kW .

content: Saturated steam at $${100^ \circ }C$$ condenses on the outside of a tube. Cold fluid enters the tube at $${20^ \circ }C$$ and exits at $${50^ \circ }C.$$ The value of the Log Mean Temperature Difference $$(LMTD)$$ is _________ $$^ \circ C.$$

For a heat exchanger, $$\Delta {T_{\max }}$$ is the maximum temperature difference and $$\Delta {T_{\min }}$$ is the minimum temperature difference between the two fluids. $$LMTD$$ is the log mean temperature difference. $${C_{\min }}$$ and $${C_{\max }}$$ are the minimum and the maximum heat capacity rates. The maximum possible heat transfer $$\left( {{Q_{\max }}} \right)$$ between the two fluids is

In a condenser of a power plant, the steam condenses at a temperature of $${60^ \circ }C.$$ The cooling water enters at $${30^ \circ }C$$ and leaves at $${45^ \circ }C.$$ The logarithmic mean temperature difference $$(LMTD)$$ of the condenser is
For the same inlet and outlet temperatures of hot and cold fluids, the Log mean temperature Difference $$(LMTD)$$ is

options.content: greater for parallel flow heat exchanger than for counter flow heat exchanger.
greater for counter flow heat exchanger than for parallel flow heat exchanger.
same for both parallel and counter flow heat exchangers.
dependent on the properties of the fluids.

content: The practice to use steam on the shell side and cooling water on the tube side in condensers of steam power plant is because

options.content: to increase overall heat transfer coefficient water side velocity can be increased if water is on the tube side
condenser can act as a storage unit for condensed steam
rate of condensation of steam is invariably smaller than the mass flow rate of cooling water
it is easier to maintain vacuum on the shell side than on the tube side

content: In shell and tube heat exchanger, baffles are mainly used to

options.content: deflect the flow in desired direction
Reduce fouling of the tube surface .

content: A condenser is used as a heat exchanger in a large steam power plant in which steam is condensed to liquid water. The condenser is a shell and tube heat exchanger which consists of 1 shell and 20,000 tubes. Water flows through each of the tubes at a rate of 1 kg/s with an inlet temperature of 30 °C. The steam in the condenser shell condenses at the rate of 430 kg/s at a temperature of 50 °C. If the heat of vaporization is 2.326 MJ/kg and specific heat of water is 4 kJ/(kg.K), the effectiveness of the heat exchanger is _______ (rounded off to 3 decimal places).
explanation:
To find the effectiveness of the heat exchanger, we first need to determine the heat transfer from the steam to the water flowing through the tubes.

The heat transfer rate from the steam is calculated using the heat of vaporization:

$$ Q_s = \dot{m}_s \cdot h_{fg} $$

Where:

$$ \dot{m}_s $$ is the mass flow rate of steam, which is 430 kg/s.

$$ h_{fg} $$ is the heat of vaporization, which is 2.326 MJ/kg (or 2326 kJ/kg).

So:

$$ Q_s = 430 \, \text{kg/s} \, \times \, 2326 \, \text{kJ/kg} = 1000180 \, \text{kW} $$

The heat transfer rate to the cooling water in the tubes is given by:

$$ Q_w = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T $$

Where:

$$ \dot{m}_w $$ is the mass flow rate of water through all tubes combined. Given that each tube has a flow rate of 1 kg/s and there are 20,000 tubes:

$$ \dot{m}_w = 20,000 \, \text{tubes} \, \times \, 1 \, \text{kg/s/tube} = 20,000 \, \text{kg/s} $$

$$ c_p $$ is the specific heat capacity of water, which is 4 kJ/(kg.K).

$$ \Delta T $$ is the temperature change of water.

The heat transfer rate from the steam must equal the heat transfer rate to the water:

$$ Q_s = Q_w $$

Thus:

$$ 1000180 \, \text{kW} = 20,000 \, \text{kg/s} \, \times \, 4 \, \text{kJ/(kg.K)} \, \times \, \Delta T $$

$$ \Delta T = \frac{1000180 \, \text{kW}}{20000 \, \times \, 4 \, \text{kJ/(kg.K)}} = 12.50225 \, \text{K} $$

The exit temperature of the water:

$$ T_{\text{outlet}} = T_{\text{inlet}} + \Delta T $$

$$ T_{\text{outlet}} = 30 \, ^\circ \text{C} + 12.50225 \, ^\circ \text{C} = 42.50225 \, ^\circ \text{C} $$

The maximum possible temperature change of the water $$ \Delta T_{\text{max}} $$ is the difference between the condensing steam temperature and the inlet water temperature:

$$ \Delta T_{\text{max}} = 50 \, ^\circ \text{C} - 30 \, ^\circ \text{C} = 20 \, ^\circ \text{C} $$

Effectiveness $$ \varepsilon $$ of the heat exchanger is defined as the ratio of the actual heat transfer rate to the maximum possible heat transfer rate:

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta T_{\text{max}}} $$

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{12.50225 \, ^\circ \text{C}}{20 \, ^\circ \text{C}} = 0.625 $$

Therefore, the effectiveness of the heat exchanger is:

$$ \varepsilon = 0.625 $$

content: Saturated vapor at 200 °C condenses to saturated liquid at the rate of 150 kg/s on the shell side of a heat exchanger (enthalpy of condensation h<sub>fg</sub> = 2400 kJ/kg). A fluid with C<sub>p</sub> = 4 kJ-kg<sup>-1</sup>K<sup>-1</sup>. enters at 100 °C on the tube side. If the effectiveness of the heat exchanger is 0.9, then the mass flow rate of the fluid in the tube side is __________ kg/s (in integer).
explanation:
Given :

$ \varepsilon = 0.9 $

$ \dot{m}_{\text{cond}} = 150 \text{ kg/s} $

$ h_{fg} = 2400 \text{ kJ/kg} $

Heat released :

$ \text{Heat released} = \dot{m}_{\text{cond}} \times h_{fg} $

$ \text{Heat released} = 150 \times 2400 $

Since the heat released by the condensing vapor is absorbed by the cold fluid :

$ 150 \times 2400 = \dot{m}_{CF} \times (C_p)_{CF} \times (T_F - T_i) \quad \text{(i)} $

For condensation :

$ \Delta T \text{ for condensing fluid} \rightarrow 0 $

$ \dot{m}c \rightarrow \infty $

Therefore, for the cold fluid $ \rightarrow (\dot{m}C) $ will be minimum.

Calculating the exit temperature of the cold fluid ($ T_{C_e} $) :

$ \varepsilon = \frac{T_{C_e} - T_{C_i}}{T_{h_i} - T_{C_i}} $

$ 0.9 = \frac{T_{C_e} - 100}{200 - 100} $

$ T_{C_e} = 190 $

From equation (i) :

$ 150 \times 2400 = \dot{m}_{CF} \times 4 \times (190 - 100) \left(\because T_F = T_{C_e} \text{ and } T_i = T_{C_i}\right) $

$ \dot{m}_{CF} = 1000 \text{ kg/s} $

Hence, the mass flow rate of the fluid in the tube side is 1000 kg/s.

content:
During open-heart surgery, a patient’s blood is cooled down to 25 °C from 37 °C using a concentric tube counter-flow heat exchanger. Water enters the heat exchanger at 4 °C and leaves at 18 °C. Blood flow rate during the surgery is 5 L/minute.

Use the following fluid properties :

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \text { Fluid } & \text { Density }\left(\mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^3\right) & \text { Specific heat }(\mathrm{J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K}) \\ \hline \text { Blood } & 1050 & 3740 \\ \hline \text { Water } & 1000 & 4200 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Effectiveness of the heat exchanger is _________ (round off to 2 decimal places).
explanation:
To determine the effectiveness of the heat exchanger (ε), let's start with the given temperatures :

  • Inlet temperature of hot fluid (blood), $ T_{b_i} = 37^\circ \text{C} $

  • Outlet temperature of hot fluid (blood), $ T_{h_v} = 25^\circ \text{C} $

  • Inlet temperature of cold fluid (water), $ T_{C_i} = 4^\circ \text{C} $

  • Outlet temperature of cold fluid (water), $ T_{C_c} = 18^\circ \text{C} $

The effectiveness (ε) is calculated using the formula :

$ \epsilon = \frac{C_c (T_{c_2} - T_{c_1})}{C_{\min} (T_{h_1} - T_{C_1})} $

First, calculate the mass flow rate of blood, $ \dot{m}_h $ :

$ \dot{m}_h = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}}{60 \; \text{sec}} \times 1050 \; \text{kg/m}^3 = 0.0875 \; \text{kg/sec} $

Using the energy balance equation :

$ \dot{m}_h C_h (T_{h_1} - T_{h_c}) = \dot{m}_c C_c (T_{c_c} - T_{c_i}) $

Substituting values :

$ 0.0875 \times 3740 \times 12 = \dot{m}_c \times 4200 \times 14 $

Solving for $ \dot{m}_c $ :

$ \dot{m}_c = 0.667 \; \text{kg/sec} $

Considering $ C_c = C_{\min} $, the effectiveness is :

$ \epsilon = \frac{18 - 4}{37 - 4} = 0.4242 $

Thus, the effectiveness of the heat exchanger is 0.42 (rounded to two decimal places).

content: In a counter-flow heat exchanger, water is heated at the rate of $$1.5kg/s$$ from $$\,40{}^ \circ C\,$$ to $$\,80{}^ \circ C\,$$ by an oil entering at $$\,120{}^ \circ C\,$$ and leaving at $$\,60{}^ \circ C\,$$. The specific heats of water and oil are $$4.2kJ/kgK$$ and $$2kJ/kgK,$$ respectively. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $$400\,W/{m^2}K.$$ The required heat transfer surface are (in $${m^2}$$) is

For the cold stream in both the heat exchangers, the flow rate and the inlet temperature are $$2$$ $$kg/s$$ and $$10{}^ \circ C$$, respectively. The hot and cold streams in both the heat exchangers are of the same fluid. Also, both the heat exchangers have the same overall heat transfer coefficient. The ratio $${A_c}/{A_p}$$ is ______________

The engine oil enters at $${100^ \circ }C$$ and flows in the annular flow passage. The exit temperature of the engine oil is $${50^ \circ }C.$$ Mass flow rate of water and the engine oil are $$1.5kg/s$$ and $$1$$ $$kg/s,$$ respectively. The specific heat of water and oil are $$4178$$ $$J/kg.K$$ and $$2130$$ $$J/kg.K,$$ respectively. The effectiveness of this heat exchanger is _________.

Shell side water at $$25$$ liter/s is entering  at $${46^ \circ }C$$. Assume constant properties of water; density is $$1000\,kg/{m^3}$$ and specific heat is $$4186$$ $$J/kg.K.$$ The $$LMTD$$ (in $${}^ \circ C$$) is ____________.

Neglecting heat transfer to the surroundings, the outlet temperature of the cold fluid in $$ = {^ \circ }C$$ is

Two fluids, $$A$$ and $$B$$ exchange heat in a counter-current heat exchanger. Fluid $$A$$ enters at $${420^ \circ }C$$ and has a mass flow rate of $$1$$ $$kg/s.$$ Fluid $$B$$ enters at $${20^ \circ }C$$ and also has a mass flow rate of $$1$$ $$kg/s,$$ Effectiveness of heat exchanger is $$75\% $$. Determine the heat transfer rate and exit temperature of fluid $$B.$$ (Specific heat of fluid $$A$$ is $$1$$ $$kJ/kg$$ $$K$$ and that of fluid $$B$$ is $$4$$ $$kJ/kgK$$).

Hot water flows with a velocity of $$0.1$$ $$m/s$$ in a $$100mm$$ long, $$0.1m$$ diameter pipe. Heat lost from the pipe outer wall is uniform and equal to $$420$$ $$W/{m^2}$$. If the inlet water temperature is $${80^ \circ }C,$$ calculate the water temp at the exit. Neglect effect of pipe wall thickness. $${C_p}$$ (water) $$=4.2$$ $$kJ/kg$$-$$K$$ and density of water $$=1000$$ $$kg/\,{m^3}.$$

A hot fluid at $${200^ \circ }C$$ enters a heat exchanger at a mass flow rate of $${10^4}\,\,kg/hr.$$ Its specific heat is $$2000$$ $$J/kg$$-$$K.$$ It is to be cooled by another fluid entering at $${25^ \circ }C$$ with a mass flow rate $$2500$$ $$kg/hr$$ and specific heat $$400$$ $$J/kg$$-$$K$$. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on outside area of $$20\,\,{m^2}$$ is $$250\,\,\,W/{m^2}K.$$ Find the exit temperature of the hot fluid when the fluids are in parallel flow.

In a certain double pipe heat exchanger hot water flows at a rate of $$50,000$$ $$kg/h$$ and gets cooled from $${95^ \circ }C$$ to $${65^ \circ }C$$. At the same time $$50,000$$ $$kg/h$$ of cooling water at $${30^ \circ }C$$ enters the heat exchanger. The flow conditions are such that the overall heat transfer coefficient remains constant at $$2270$$ $$W/{m^2}K$$. Calculate the heat transfer area required, assuming the two streams are in parallel flow, and for both the streams $${C_p} = 4.2\,\,kJ/kg\,\,K$$

A counter flow heat exchanger is to heat air entering at $${400^ \circ }C$$ with a flow rate of $$6$$ $$kg/s$$ by the exhaust gas entering at $${800^ \circ }C$$ with a flow rate of $$4$$ $$kg/s$$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $${100^ \circ }C$$ $$W/\left( {{m^2}K} \right)$$ and the outlet temperature of the air is $${551.5^ \circ }C.$$ Specific heat at constant pressure for both air and the exhaust gas can be taken as $$1100$$ $$J/(kgK).$$ Calculate the heat transfer area needed and the number of transfer units.
answer:
Heat transfer area $$ = 47.99\,{m^2}$$
No of transfer unit $$=1.09$$

content: Two streams of fluid of unit constant specific heats and unit mass flow rate exchange thermal energy in an adiabatic heat exchanger. The inlet temps of hot and cold streams are $${300^ \circ }C$$ and $${30^ \circ }C$$ respectively. Calculate the $$LMTD$$ and effectiveness of the heat exchanger if the hot fluid is cooled to zero entropy condition.

A one shell pass, one tube-pass heat exchanger, has counter flow configuration between the shell side and tube side fluids. The total number of tubes within the heat exchanger is $$10$$ and the tube dimensions are $$ID=10$$ $$mm,$$ $$OD=12$$ $$mm$$ and length $$=1m.$$ saturated dry steam enters the shell side at a flow rate of $$2$$ $$kg/s$$ and the temp of $${100^ \circ }C.$$ In the tube side, cold water enters at a flow rate of $$10$$ $$kg/s$$ with an inlet temp of $${25^ \circ }C.$$ The $$OHTC$$ based on the outer surface area of the tubes is $$50$$ $$W/{m^2}K.$$ The specific heat of water is $$4.18$$ $$kJ/kg$$-$$K$$ and the latent heat of steam is $$2500$$ $$kJ/kg.$$ What is the condition of the steam at the exit.

A double pipe counter flow heat exchanger is to be designed to cool $$12000$$ $$kg/hr$$ of an oil of specific heat $$1.95$$ $$kJ/kgK$$ from $$\,85{}^ \circ C$$ to $$\,55{}^ \circ C$$ by water entering the heat exchanger at $$\,30{}^ \circ C$$ and leaving at $$\,45{}^ \circ C$$. If the $$OHTC$$ of heat exchanger is $$400$$ $$\,W/{m^2}K.\,$$ Calculate the $$LMTD$$ and the surface area of the heat exchanger

A shell and tube heat exchanger is to be designed for heating pressurized water by means of hot gasses which get cooled the data are as follows
Temp of water at the inlet $$ = \,80{}^ \circ C$$
Temp of the water at the outlet $$ = \,140{}^ \circ C$$
Temp of hot gasses at the inlet $$ = \,340{}^ \circ C$$
Temp of hot gasses at the outlet $$ = \,180{}^ \circ C$$
Mass flow rate of water $$=12$$ $$kg/s,$$ Specific heat of water $$=4.2kJ/kgK$$
$$OHTC=30$$ $$W/{m^2}K$$,
Correction factor for $$LMTD$$ based on counter flow conditions $$=0.9$$
Calculate the tube surface area required in the heat exchanger and the effectiveness of the heat exchanger.
